other_observers: None at the time, but others (including Peter Gent) later saw the bird.; Yes
specific_location: Just off the North Shanahan Trail in the Flatirons, about 1/3 mile in from the trailhead.
description: The most distinctive feature of this bird was its song, with rising inflections on the "Pee" and "wee." This is what first caught my attention as I was hiking along the nearby trail.   It had a similar size and shape to a Western Wood-Pewee (which was common in the area), with an erect posture, slender build, no eye ring, and buffy wingbars. This bird, however, had a complete, bright orange lower mandible, and pale whitish throat and breast. No dark markings were visible on the breast, as viewed in bright light.Behaviors: The bird was very active, flying around a circle of 200'-300' diameter, perching 10'-15' up in ponderosa pines, singing once or twice, and then flying on.Call: "Pee-wee" or "Pee-a-wee" song, repeated numerous timesPlumage: adult
